We had salt pork and hardtack for dinner—nothing left to eat but that until we reach the next fort.
The coffee was weak, but after days of drinking water from the river, it felt like a luxury.
We had no choice but to eat cornmeal mush and hope for a better meal tomorrow.
A couple of us managed to catch some rabbits, but the rest of the group had to settle for dried beef and hardtack.
Our supplies are running low. We’re down to just flour, water, and a little bit of salt pork left to get us to the next station.
The biscuits are turning moldy. We’re having a hard time keeping things dry, and the hardtack doesn’t taste much better after three weeks.
At last, we found a patch of wild berries, and we made a berry pie over the campfire.
Everyone is complaining about the sour bread and salt pork, but it’s the only food we have until the next town.
We had to make do with beans for supper tonight—dried beans soaked in water and boiled with some bacon fat.
The heat of the sun dried out the cornmeal, but we cooked it up into cornbread, and it was a welcome change.
We traded a couple of oxen for some dried apples at the last fort—better than the hardtack we’ve been eating for weeks.
We’ve had the same soup for three days now, made from whatever we could catch and add from the pack.
It’s been a rough stretch. We’ve gone for days without anything but salted beef and water, and the children are hungry.
For the first time in days, we were able to roast venison we caught along the trail. It felt like a feast.
We’ve run out of coffee, but the hot water tastes better than nothing at all.
After a long week of traveling, we finally ate some fresh bread baked in the fire—a small victory.
Supper was nothing but cornmeal boiled with water—a dry, bland meal, but it filled us up.
We found some wild onions on the trail today, and the cook made a stew with the last of our salt pork.
The biscuits were soggy and stale, but we had no choice but to eat them—they’re better than starving.
Tonight, we had some jerky and dried beans—not much, but we’re thankful for any meal at all.
